title: Financial Intelligence
subtitle: Money, Investing, and Wealth Mindset
description: An advanced checklist for those who have already mastered basic expense tracking and want to move on to capital accumulation. Here are key principles of behavioral finance, investment strategies, and the psychology of money from leading economists and investors worldwide.

items.title: The 50/30/20 Rule
items.text: A balanced system for allocating income: 50% to needs (housing, food), 30% to wants (entertainment, hobbies), and 20% to savings and investments. This isn't a strict diet but a sustainable framework allowing you to live now and build the future simultaneously.
items.tip: Calculate your percentages from last month's net income. Chances are, proportions are skewed toward 'wants.'

items.title: Emergency Fund 2.0
items.source: Morgan Housel — The Psychology of Money
items.text: The classic recommendation is 3-6 months of expenses. But true psychological freedom comes with a 12-month cushion. This provides the ability to quit an unfulfilling job, take a break for retraining, or weather a crisis without panic-selling assets.
items.tip: Keep your emergency fund in highly liquid instruments: savings account, money market funds, short-term government bonds.

items.title: Loss Aversion
items.source: Daniel Kahneman & Amos Tversky — Prospect Theory
items.text: The pain of losing 1000 rubles is 2-2.5 times stronger than the pleasure of finding 1000 rubles. This bias causes investors to sell assets in a panic (locking in losses) and hold losing positions too long, hoping to 'break even.'
items.tip: Before buying an asset, write down: 'Under what conditions will I sell this security?' This reduces emotional decision-making.

items.title: Diversification vs. Concentration
items.text: Diversification is the only 'free lunch' in investing. By reducing portfolio risk, you don't necessarily reduce returns. Dalio's 'All Weather Portfolio' is built to weather any economic season: growth, recession, inflation, and deflation.
items.tip: Start with asset class allocation: stocks, bonds, gold — not choosing specific companies.

items.title: Compound Interest and Time
items.text: The investor's main asset is not money or intelligence, but time. 10,000 rubles invested at 15% per year becomes 660,000 rubles after 30 years without any extra effort. But 80% of this growth occurs in the last 10 years.
items.tip: Start investing any amount right now. Time in the market is more important than timing the market.

items.title: Mental Accounting
items.text: We treat money differently depending on its source. Bonuses or winnings are spent more easily than salary. Investors often risk 'profits' (house money) but are afraid to touch the 'principal.' Rationally, it's the same money.
items.tip: Treat all money as a single capital pool. Don't create mental 'wallets' for 'rainy day' and 'fun.'

items.title: Business vs. Speculation
items.text: An investment operation is one which, upon thorough analysis, promises safety of principal and an adequate return. Everything else is speculation. When buying a stock, think like an owner of the business, not a gambler guessing quotes.
items.tip: Read the annual report of the company whose stock you want to buy. If you don't understand how it makes money — don't buy.

items.title: Inflation: The Silent Thief
items.text: Inflation at 8% eats half the purchasing power of money in 9 years. Storing large sums 'under the mattress' or in a checking account is a guaranteed loss. Money must work to preserve its value.
items.tip: Calculate real return using: Nominal return minus inflation.

items.title: Financial Plan and Goals
items.text: Investing without a goal is like a trip without a destination. Market volatility scares only when you don't know why you need the money and when. The investment horizon determines the acceptable risk level.
items.tip: Split your portfolio into 'baskets': money needed in 2 years (deposits/bonds), in 10 years (stocks), in 30 years (higher risk assets).

items.title: Dividends Are Not Free Money
items.text: A common misconception: dividend stocks are better. In reality, after a dividend is paid, the stock price falls exactly by the dividend amount (dividend gap). The company simply transfers some of your own money from your left pocket to your right, withholding tax.
items.tip: Evaluate a company by total return (capital appreciation + dividends), not just dividend yield.

items.title: Dollar-Cost Averaging
items.source: Burton Malkiel — A Random Walk Down Wall Street
items.text: Trying to catch the market bottom is futile. The strategy of regularly investing a fixed amount (DCA) allows you to buy more shares when the market falls and fewer when it rises, lowering the average purchase price.
items.tip: Set up an auto-payment to buy an index fund on the same date each month.

items.title: Endowment Effect
items.text: We overvalue what we already own. Stocks bought a year ago seem 'good' just because they're ours. This prevents objective assessment of a company's prospects and timely disposal of dead weight.
items.tip: Once a quarter, ask yourself: 'Would I buy this stock today at its current price if I didn't own it?'

items.title: Your Most Important Asset is Yourself
items.text: For most people under 35-40, their main source of future capital is their employability and skills. Investments in education, health, and professional growth yield returns of hundreds or thousands percent per year, unattainable in the stock market.
items.tip: Before looking for stocks with 20% return, think about increasing your monthly income by 20%.

items.title: Information Noise
items.text: Financial news creates an illusion of control and understanding. In reality, the more often you check quotes and read forecasts, the worse your results. Noise causes impulsive actions.
items.tip: Check your portfolio quarterly or semi-annually, not daily.

quiz.q: According to Kahneman and Tversky, what is stronger: the pain of loss or the joy of gain?
a: The pain of loss is 2-2.5 times stronger

quiz.q: What is the 'only free lunch' in investing?
What percentage of income does the 50/30/20 rule recommend allocating to savings?
What happens to a stock's price on the day after the dividend record date (ex-dividend date)?
What is the main idea of the Dollar-Cost Averaging (DCA) strategy?
a: Buy regularly with a fixed sum
Sell every time at the peak

quiz.q: Which asset do N. Taleb and M. Housel consider most important for a young person?
What is the danger of 'Mental Accounting'?
